text_clean: Es un parque en una ciudad, ¿por qué hay que pagar para entrar?, ¿no llega con los impuestos de los limeños? Hay que pagar 5 soles por persona, incluso un niño de 5 años Además dentro hay muchas atracciones y en todas hay que pagar, incluso para ir al baño cobran El colmo es que hay bastantes zonas cerradas: el paseo por las columnas, la parte más próxima a la fuente de colores, las cercanías de la casa como de cuento, vallas también cerca de las mariposas... El espectáculo de luces y agua es bonito pero del resto, me llevé una gran decepción

text_clean: Nasca es con " S" las culturas del antiguo Perú no conocieron la "Z". Las Líneas de Nasca son Patrimonio Cultural de la humanidad y es la obra visual más compleja que para admirarla solo es posible volando. Es un plano con geometría compleja, figuras estilizadas como señales y acertijos para interpretar y descubrir. Mucho se debe a la Dra. María Reiche por dedicar su vida entera a Las Líneas de NASCA.

text_clean: English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

text_clean: Las piletas y el shows estuvo muy agradable e interesante el problema es que hay demasiados atracciones con costo adicional y eso no es tan agradable ya que a cada rato aumenta la inversión de la visita al lugar. Una de las cosas que mas me disgusto fue que para entrar al túnel subterráneo, ahora cobran adicional cuando anteriormente el pase era libre a todas las instalaciones del parque. Otro detalle es que esos carros con muchas luces que alquilan en el lugar, casi atropellan a un niño, super peligroso. No deberian estar ahi, otra cosa es que hay demasiados juegos y le quitan protagonismo a la esencia del parque, ya que antes los niños iban a divertirse con el agua, ahora lo único que les llama la atencion es la cantidad de juegos que han puesto por todo lugar.

Areas like Miraflores and Barranco felt more secure than the city center.

Audio guides would be the perfect solution.

text_clean: Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

text_clean: Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: Fui al circuito 1-C el 17 de abril a las 8:00 am y me tocó lluvia y niebla. No obstante despeja por momentos y se puede apreciar la vista desde la casa del guardian al que se tiene acceso, pero por la condición climática descarté la subida que ofrecía la ruta porque no valía la pena. Mejor reservar para entrar al medio día, vas a la fija. Aunque el paisaje es encantador por las montañas, incluso desde el tren, me pareció que tanto sacrificio y dinero para llegar hasta allí está un poco sobrevalorado. Demasiados turistas.

text_clean: Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?
